question: Define an entity set and list the three main structural constraints used in the Entity-Relationship (ER) model.
expectedPoints: An entity set is a collection of entities of the same type that share the same properties or attributes.
The three main structural constraints are: 1) Mapping cardinalities (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many).
3) Key constraints (primary keys that uniquely identify an entity).

question: Explain the difference between total and partial participation constraints with a clear example for each. Also, describe how these are represented in an ER diagram.
expectedPoints: Total participation means every entity in an entity set must participate in the relationship. Example: Every STUDENT must be enrolled in at least one COURSE.
Partial participation means that not every entity in an entity set is required to participate in the relationship. Example: Not every EMPLOYEE manages a PROJECT; some might just work on one.
In an ER diagram, total participation is represented by a double line connecting the entity set to the relationship, while partial participation is represented by a single line.

question: Compare and contrast the concepts of a superkey, a candidate key, and a primary key. Furthermore, analyze a practical scenario involving a weak entity set: describe its attributes, its identifying relationship, and why a partial key is necessary.
expectedPoints: A superkey is a set of one or more attributes that, taken collectively, uniquely identify an entity. A candidate key is a minimal superkey. The primary key is the candidate key chosen by the database designer to uniquely identify entities.
A weak entity set is an entity set that does not have sufficient attributes to form a primary key on its own. Its existence is dependent on a strong entity set via an identifying relationship.

A weak entity set's primary key is formed by combining the primary key of its owner strong entity set with its own partial key (discriminator). This is necessary because the weak entity's partial key is only unique relative to its owner entity. Example: The DEPENDENT entity is weak, relying on an EMPLOYEE. The partial key `dname` (dependent name) is only unique per employee, so the full primary key is `(emp_id, dname)`.
